summary:
The bronze masks of [[event:1870|Sanxingdui]] are among the most enigmatic and visually distinctive artifacts to survive from the ancient world, created by the Sanxingdui culture of Sichuan province in southwestern China around 1250–1150 BC. The masks range from small face-sized examples to enormous pieces nearly a meter wide, and are characterized by highly stylized, protruding eyes — sometimes in the form of cylindrical protrusions extending up to 16 centimeters from the face — along with enlarged ears, wide mouths with upturned corners, and often gold foil overlays on the face. The alien quality of their aesthetic has made them icons of ancient mystery.

The masks were discovered in ritual deposit pits first excavated in 1986, where they had been deliberately broken and deposited alongside other bronze objects, jade items, and ivory. The scale and quality of the metalwork indicates that the [[event:1842|bronze casting]] technology of Sanxingdui was independently sophisticated, likely using piece-mold techniques similar to those of the Shang dynasty but producing radically different artistic forms. No clear iconographic parallels to the masks exist in any contemporaneous culture, suggesting that Sanxingdui developed its religious art in substantial isolation from the Yellow River civilizations.

The function of the masks is unknown. They may represent deities, ancestors, supernatural beings, or shamanic spirits. Their exaggerated eyes have been interpreted as depicting the ability to see into spiritual realms. The fact that the objects were ritually deposited — often burned and broken — in deliberately created pits suggests they played central roles in religious ceremonies whose nature remains opaque in the absence of written records.
